title: Random sampling of self-avoiding theta-graphs
authors: Nicholas R. Beaton, Aleksander L. Owczarek
abstract: Theta-graphs are a type of spatial graph with two vertices connected by three edges. We investigate embeddings of theta-graphs in the square and simple cubic lattices, using a combination of the Wang-Landau Monte Carlo method with a variant of the BFACF algorithm which accommodates vertices of degree 3. This allows us to estimate the critical exponents governing the number of theta-graphs and the distributions of the different arm-lengths. For the cubic lattice these values can be compared to the corresponding exponents for prime knots. We also study the number of `monodisperse' theta-graphs where the three arms have the same lengths, and find evidence supporting a conjecture for the critical exponent in two dimensions.
summary: This paper uses advanced Monte Carlo techniques to study the topological and scaling properties of self-avoiding theta-graphs. It provides high-precision estimates for critical exponents and supports long-standing theoretical conjectures regarding polymer networks in two dimensions.
main_problem: The study aims to characterize the scaling behavior, critical exponents, and geometric properties of self-avoiding theta-graphs (spatial graphs with two vertices of degree 3) on 2D square and 3D cubic lattices.
main_result: The authors found evidence supporting Duplantier's conjecture for the entropic critical exponent in 2D monodisperse theta-graphs and provided precise estimates for exponents governing arm-length distributions and branch point separation.
method: A combination of the Wang-Landau Monte Carlo algorithm for density of states estimation and a variant of the BFACF algorithm adapted for degree-3 vertices was used.
model_or_system: The model consists of self-avoiding theta-graphs (polydisperse and monodisperse) embedded in 2D square and 3D simple cubic lattices, focusing on unknotted configurations in 3D.
key_observables: Critical exponents (zeta, beta, sigma, tau, eta, nu), growth rate (mu), arm-length distributions, and mean squared distance between branch points.
important_parameters: Total graph size (n), arm lengths (l1, l2, l1+l2), and lattice dimensionality (2D vs 3D).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on unknotted theta-graphs in 3D and assumes ergodicity within the expanded BFACF move set.
figures_summary: Figures include lattice topology illustrations, examples of theta-graphs, schematics of BFACF moves, and various plots of scaling ratios and error convergence used to estimate critical exponents.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the theta-graph model, describes the modified BFACF and Wang-Landau algorithms, presents numerical results for 2D and 3D scaling exponents, and concludes with comparisons to theoretical conjectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) A star, (b) a watermelon, (c) a comb, (d) a tadpole, and (e) a dumbbell.
Figure 2: A size 500 theta on the square lattice, with arm lengths 18, 66 and 416.
Figure 3: (a) and (b) Two thetas with different distributions of arm lengths. (c) A trefoil knot with a localised knot component. (d) A theta with non-trivial ‘knot’ type.
Figure 4: The basic BFACF moves for the square and cubic lattices.

Figure 5: The set of BFACF moves for spatial graphs on the cubic lattice. The moves in (a) do not change the location of the vertices of degree 3, while the moves in (b) do move at least one of the degree 3 vertices. These are in addition to the regular BFACF moves in Figure 4 (a). This figure is adapted from [51, Fig. 4.1].
